question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of times (I) his Chief Veterinary Officer has been contacted directly by his counterpart in Scotland; and (ii) he has been made aware of not for slaughter sheep imports to Northern Ireland, in the last 12 months.
answer_text: The Chief Veterinary Officer is in regular contact with his counterpart in Scotland through both individual conversations and also as part of the four Nations CVO updates. With regards to point (ii), in line with the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024, imports to Northern Ireland of not for slaughter sheep fall under the direction and control of the Secretary of State for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline any commitments that can be given that their Department will carry out work to improve the application process for the Troubles Permanent Disablement Payment Scheme.
answer_text: To date, the Victims’ Payments Board (VPB) has received 12,974 applications and has paid over £133 million to eligible applicants since the Scheme opened for applications on 31 August 2021.   However, we acknowledge and understand the concerns that have been raised regarding the time it can take and welcome steps that have been taken by VPB to streamline the process and enhance the service provided following a review.It is encouraging to note that the VPB has now established a dedicated Business Transformation Project Team, with the aim of delivering more efficient and responsive support to those applying to the Scheme. This includes an Operational Process Review, which has already led to an increase in the number of assessments being returned on a monthly basis through a number of initiatives.With the Scheme set to close for new applications at the end of August 2026, we strongly encourage anyone who may be eligible to submit an application before the deadline.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on the current position with the ongoing industrial action by the Criminal Bar Association.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on capital works for strengthening Ardtrea Bridge, Moneymore.
answer_text: The Member will be aware from correspondence with officials, that Ardtrea Bridge is being progressed as a bridge replacement scheme and that further design review work is underway. While this has inevitably delayed progress, it has been necessary to reassess the proposed design against updated flood risk guidance.The reassessment has included additional hydraulic and flood modelling to ensure compliance with the current requirements. This work is essential to ensure the scheme is resilient and capable of securing the necessary approvals to proceed.Until this technical review is concluded, the Department cannot confirm a final programme for procurement or construction, as the outcome will determine whether further design amendments are required.I remain committed to progressing the Ardtrea Bridge scheme as quickly as practicable and will continue to keep the Member informed as the outstanding issues are resolved.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on legislation to establish a managing authority for controlled schools.
To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on how the £81 million funding to be provided by HM Treasury for support with energy bills will be used in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My Department, working with the Department of Finance, had secured a commitment from Treasury that it would cover 75% of the Northern Ireland Renewables Obligation, the NIRO, cost to consumers, consistent with the support announced for British electricity consumers in the Autumn Budget. This amounts to around £30 discount on domestic electricity bills per household per year, for three years, which total is around £81m. The British Government is extending the powers for the North within the Energy Prices Act 2022 in the Westminster Parliament, which will enable us to pass on this discount from 1st July.

answer_text: In line with the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024, the movement of animals to Northern Ireland, including imports of not-for-slaughter sheep, falls under the Direction and Control of the Secretary of State for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs.

The assessment of submitted applications is well underway, and applicants will be notified of the outcome of their application by the end of April 2026.This very high level of interest in the Fund indicates a clear and ongoing need for this type of support within the voluntary and community sector.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission for an update on security provision around Parliament Buildings.
answer_text: The Assembly Commission fully acknowledges the importance of maintaining a balance between security and ensuring that the work of the Assembly is readily accessible to the public. In light of that, Assembly Officials keep security at Parliament Buildings under close review and maintain regular liaison with relevant organisations and external bodies.Officials also closely monitor security related incidents or developments throughout Northern Ireland and beyond, as these can help in determining operational measures and procedures relative to the security profile at Parliament Buildings. That includes monitoring terrorist threat levels within the wider UK and specifically Northern Ireland, and these threat levels currently sit at substantial which is the middle of five tiers.For security reasons, the Assembly Commission does not typically discuss specific security measures.  However Assembly Officials are scoping recommendations made by PSNI in recent security reviews both in terms of feasibility and costs.One key initiative recently taken forward by Assembly Officials has been the provision of on-line training known as SCaN which is aimed at improving general security awareness, and which has now been made mandatory for all Assembly Commission staff. That training has been supplemented with classroom training provided by PSNI, to front-line staff.  This training has been very well received by participants and PSNI have indicated that they would be prepared to provide this training to other users of Parliament Buildings if desired.  Members will be provided with information on this shortly.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when the Moyola River feasibility study will be published.
answer_text: My Department’s Rivers Directorate continues to progress a feasibility study to assess the river flood risk to properties within the Moyola catchment in Mid Ulster. This work will identify if there are any viable measures to reduce the impacts of flooding from the Moyola River in the key towns of Maghera, Tobermore, Castledawson, Magherafelt and the surrounding rural areas. The feasibility study is nearing completion and is currently estimated to be finalised in Summer 2026.  This feasibility study will fully assess the practicality, suitability, benefit, costs and potential impacts of providing flood alleviation options in the Moyola catchment. As part of the Department’s drive to deliver wholly sustainable solutions, additional work is being undertaken in this study to assess the potential for the inclusion of natural flood management techniques. Potential solutions will be identified and assessed for suitability as part of any possible viable final solution.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the Waiting List Reimbursement Scheme.
answer_text: The Waiting List Reimbursement Scheme is operating effectively, with efficient processing times and high patient satisfaction among those who meet the eligibility criteria.I can report that, in the 8 month period since its launch on 2 June 2025 up to 02 February 2026, a total of 500 applications have been successfully prior-approved to seek treatment outside Northern Ireland and to claim reimbursement up to the Health and Social Care equivalent cost.  Total expenditure to date is £724k, against an estimated financial commitment of £4.63m associated with all approved applications.  £10m was allocated to the scheme on a non-recurrent basis, however, any funding not required in-year is redirected to other priority areas within the Elective Care Framework. This demand led approach ensures that all available resources are used to best effect, supporting patient care, addressing long waits for appointments and operations and maximising impact on waiting list reduction. The Waiting List Reimbursement Scheme is one of a range of initiatives being taken forward by my Department as part of its wider programme of elective care interventions to tackle waiting lists and improve timely access to treatment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any packages of support his Department will offer farmers to address damage to lands, drainage, fencing and habitats following flooding caused by unseasonal levels of rain.
answer_text: At this stage, a support package for farmers to address issues caused by flooding is not planned, however I am continuing to monitor the situation.	The Sustainable Agriculture Programme’s Farm Sustainability Payment Scheme is designed to improve the resilience of farm businesses and help withstand ‘shocks’ that are beyond their ability to manage effectively, encouraging businesses to become more environmentally sustainable and efficient.		A Crisis Framework is being developed as part of the Sustainable Agriculture Programme (SAP). This framework will provide a pre-defined approach for government intervention in the event of a significant crisis impacting the agricultural sector in Northern Ireland.		The SAP is a strong example of climate adaptation action being progressed by my Department, as we work with and support our agricultural sector to be more resilient to the more frequent and severe impacts brought by climate change. As such, delivery of the SAP is a key action in the draft Northern Ireland’s third Climate Change Adaptation Programme (NICCAP3), which my Department has developed, with input from all Departments. 		As a cross-cutting Programme with more than 280 actions to build climate resilience, the NICCAP3 requires Executive agreement. I am currently engaging with my Executive colleagues to seek their agreement to finalise NICCAP3 and lay it in this Assembly as soon as possible.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

answer_text: The active travel scheme on the A29 Dungannon Road was appraised in April 2021 in line with the procedures that were in place prior to the development of the Active Travel Delivery Plan assessment process.  The scheme seeks to connect existing active travel infrastructure in Cookstown to the CAFRE Loughry College campus, Mid Ulster Sports Arena, local businesses and amenities in the area. Future prioritisation of active travel routes within our towns and cities will be primarily in line with the emerging Active Travel Delivery Plan, which is still under consideration. question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline the budget allocated for road safety in Mid Ulster for the 2025/26 financial year.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department, and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and to address the needs of all road users. My Department’s budget covers a number of areas specific to road safety including road safety education and training, safe roads and road sides, safe speeds, safe vehicles and post collision inspection and response to collisions in partnership with the PSNI.My Department does not hold a separately ringfenced budget for “road safety” at district level, including for the Mid Ulster area.  Instead, road safety measures — such as collisionremedial works, traffic calming, pedestrian and active travel improvements, and parttime 20 mph schemes at schools — are funded from the Department’s wider Roads Resource and Capital budgets, which are allocated on a regional basis and then prioritised locally according to needMy Department’s programme of network development and highway maintenance schemes deliver both direct and indirect road safety benefits such as improved skid resistance from resurfacing and enhanced pedestrian safety through the installation of controlled crossings.It is therefore not possible to provide a specific budget for road safety within the Mid Ulster area.  However, my Department’s Annual Report to Mid Ulster Council provides full details of roads programmes with an allocated budget of £7,596,000 during the 25/26 financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the Rural Development Programme.
answer_text: My Department is currently developing a new rural policy using a co-design process involving a wide range of stakeholders following the UK’s exit from the EU and the closure of the NI Rural Development Programme 2014-2020.The three Rural Policy Co-design Working Groups established to assist with the policy development work submitted their final reports to the Department in September 2025.   I am grateful to the wide range of stakeholders who committed to and participated in this ambitious co-design process.  I have considered these reports alongside the views of the Rural Policy Co-design Oversight Group and have developed a cohesive set of outline rural policy proposals.  This important policy development work is continuing at pace, and it is my intention that a public consultation will commence on the policy proposals early next year.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline any undeveloped land owned by the Housing Executive in Mid Ulster.
answer_text: The Housing Executive has advised me that they currently maintain approximately 1.27 Hectares of land in Magherafelt District on three sites included in the Social Housing Development Programme for development by a Housing Association to meet social housing need. They also have land in six locations in Magherafelt - approximately 2.53 Hectares - which is being retained pending further investigation into its development potential for social housing or suitability for other functions carried out by the Housing Executive. The Housing Executive maintains a further approximately 2.61 hectares of land in nine locations in Mid Ulster – .79 hectares in Cookstown District and 1.82 hectares in Magherafelt District - assessed as having independent development potential subject to planning restrictions being overcome.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any regeneration schemes planned for Mid Ulster, in the next five years.

This scheme will benefit a number of towns to include Coalisland, Cookstown and Magherafelt in the Mid Ulster constituency.Any further projects that are being considered for delivery in the next five years would be subject to Business Case approval and considered within my Department’s budgetary priorities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline any action her Department has taken in relation to job creation in Mid Ulster in the last 12 months.
answer_text: In the last financial year, Invest NI supported projects that will lead to the creation of 457 new jobs within the Mid Ulster council area. 75% of the 457 new jobs will pay salaries higher than the NI Private Sector Median salary and will contribute to £17 million in annual salaries in the local economy. The 457 jobs represent 16% of all the jobs Invest NI supported in 2024/25. The benefits will also be felt across the council area with support provided to companies such as Michael Nugent in Pomeroy, P McVey Building Systems in Toomebridge and Topframe Engineering in Casteldawson.Local Economic Partnerships have been established which are developing action plans that address barriers to growth which will boost job creation, and £3.7 million has been provided to support the partnership in Mid Ulster.I also recognise the impact the cost of energy is having on competitiveness and business’s ability to create jobs. To mitigate I recently published a decision paper on Fairer Grid Connection Costs which means that homes and businesses seeking a connection to the electricity grid, will see any reinforcement costs spread over time and across customers. This will remove a major barrier to business investment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline the reason for the current delay in processing firearm appeals.
answer_text: ANSWER – AQW 2802/22-27Firearm appeals are considered on a case-by-case basis with each appeal having varying complexities that require background information to be gathered from PSNI’s Firearms and Explosives Branch (PSNI FEB) and in some cases from other external parties, such as GP’s or other health professionals.  Appellants have the opportunity to make representations and, in many cases, they request further time.  Within recent years, staffing and resource issues in both PSNI FEB and my Department’s Firearms and Explosives Branch (DoJ FEB) contributed to delays in processing firearms appeals.  Extra resources were allocated to progress older appeals to completion and reduce the backlog.  The number of live firearm appeals has been reduced by 20% in the past six months.  The appeal team are not in a position to advise on a definitive timeframe for processing a specific firearm appeal, due to the individual complexities of each appeal.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of; and (ii) location of, Changing Places toilets in hospitals, in each Health and Social Care Trust building; and (ii) whether there are any plans to make such facilities mandatory in all new builds and refurbishments.
answer_text: Below are the details of the number and location of Changing Places facilities provided by each Health and Social Care Trust.    Northern Health and Social Care Trust  The NHSCT has two Changing Places facilities with one located at Antrim Hospital and one within the Ballymena Health & Care Centre.     South Eastern Health and Care Trust  The SEHSCT has one Changing Places facility within the Lisburn Primary and Community Care Centre located on the Lagan Valley Hospital Site.  Southern Health and Social Care Trust  The SHSCT has two fully compliant Changing Places facilities with one located at the Banbridge CTCC site and one located at the Blossom Paediatrics building at Craigavon Area Hospital.     Belfast Health and Social Care Trust  The BHSCT does not yet have a Changing Place facility in any of its acute hospital sites.     Western Health and Social Care Trust   No response received.  In light of the severe financial challenges facing the Northern Ireland Health & Social Care (HSC) sector, as well as the competing demands on the capital budget, the department has no plans to centrally fund the provision of Changing Places facilities in any existing hospitals or health centres where currently not provided. HSC Trusts are however provided with general capital funding monies and may consider the provision of these facilities from this allocation based on their own assessment of need and options available.  Since June 2022 amendments to building regulations require that all new build hospitals and primary care buildings and those whole hospital or primary care buildings formed by a material change of use of existing facilities should provide at least one changing places facility.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for their assessment of the recent asylum policy reforms announced by the Prime Minister in October 2025.
To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update in relation to increased socialisation of connection costs in the electricity distribution network.
To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of Education Authority maintenance backlogs in relation to schools.
answer_text: The Education Authority is currently in year three of a five-year condition survey cycle across the schools’ estate. Based on the surveys completed to date, the maintenance backlog is circa. £250m. Following completion of the full cycle of condition surveys in 2028, it is projected that the maintenance backlog in schools will be circa. £700m - £800m.The scale of this backlog brings into sharp focus the critical and long-standing issues of school infrastructure and funding.It exposes the scale of the challenge, particularly the chronic underinvestment in our schools’ estate over many years.The key issue here is not a lack of planning, but a lack of funding. Severe and unsustainable financial pressures have left my department unable to provide the EA with sufficient funding to carry out routine maintenance or invest in long-term improvements. Instead, resources have had to be directed to cover statutory requirements and address the most urgent, health and safety-related repairs in a reactive approach that, as highlighted in the recent report from the Public Accounts Committee, cannot continue.I remain committed to improving the condition of our school estate and have called for urgent, cross-party support to secure the investment needed to ensure that every child in Northern Ireland is educated in a safe, modern, and inclusive environment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline the number of children currently awaiting a foster carer.
answer_text: From the most recent Statutory Functions data provided by the 5 HSC Trusts to DoH as of 31st March 2025 , there were a total of 3514 children in foster care placements in Northern Ireland - this includes kinship and non-kinship placements and placements with Independent Fostering Providers.In addition to this, it was reported that 135 children in care were in inappropriate placements given their assessed needs, and awaiting an appropriate foster placement. By its definition alone, an inappropriate placement for a child does not necessarily mean that the child is at risk. An inappropriate placement could be • children in the care of their parents requiring foster placements;• children in residential care requiring foster placements;• children in short term/bridging placements requiring long term fostering placements;• children accommodated in a kinship placement requiring foster placement.  children in emergency foster care who require a longer term foster placement. Foster Care numbers have grown steadily and the position at March 2025 equated to 2868 foster carers, offering 3645 fostering placements, the highest there has ever been. Kinship foster carers account for 55% of that total. There will always be children who require care outside of their family and more recently HSC Trusts have struggled to recruit new non-kin foster carers, and data evidences a 4% reduction in their numbers; the availability of non-kin carers is a significant pressure point for HSC Trusts.This issue is also impacted by the increase in numbers of Children in Care with 4188 children being in care as of 31st March 2025, an increase of 18.7% over the past 4 years.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline the amount spent by her Department in repairing road signs that include the name Londonderry over the last five years.
answer_text: My Department does not keep specific records of how traffic signs have been damaged, only that they are damaged.Damage to street furniture such as road signs may be as a result of storm damage, a Road Traffic Collision, vandalism or simply the sign may be at the end of is serviceable life.The current underfunding of road maintenance means that priority for the repair and replacement of road signs is targeted at those signs which play an important role in road safety.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) to detail how she will address the shortfall in structural road maintenance funding; and (ii) for her assessment of the Mineral Products Association Northern Ireland report that the shortfall could lead to (a) more than 500 skilled workers and apprentices losing their jobs; and (b) the loss of significant income to contractors who have made investment in recruitment, training and equipment to meet their obligations to contracts award by her Department.
answer_text: There has been historic underfunding for the maintenance of the road network for many years due to austerity by the British Government, and I am keen to improve our infrastructure with the limited funding available to me as it delivers real benefits for our citizens, our communities and the economy. My Department submits bids in line with the recommendation and has included a pragmatic ramping up approach which reflects the capacity of internal and external resources I have met recently with the MPANI to discuss the concerns of the industry, and I am aware of the concerns raised as well as the recent issues raised by the MPANI in their evidence to the DfI committee. I remain committed to seeking additional funding to address the need to invest in the road network and I understand the need for the Contracting Industry be able to investment in recruitment, training and equipment to ensure that the capacity exists to provide the much-needed improvements to the road network. There are, however, many competing financial demands across my Department and the Executive and it will be challenging to address years of underfunding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, regarding the Final Scheme Design for a Renewable Electricity Support Scheme, to detail how her Department will assure project owners that, projects that energise prior to the first auction, will remain eligible to participate.
answer_text: The Final Scheme Design for the Renewable Electricity Price Guarantee (REPG) scheme was published on 19 September 2025.My Department is exploring options to enable projects that energise prior to the first auction to remain eligible to participate. As noted in the Final Design, further analysis is required to assess the potential impacts of this approach, particularly on the 80% by 2030 target and consumer costs, and to determine appropriate eligibility dates. My department intends to consult on draft Terms and Conditions for the REPG scheme, including eligibility, later this year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline any actions that have been taken to enhance mental health services in Mid Ulster.
To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the reason for the delay of over one year to process firearm appeals in her Department.
answer_text: Firearm appeals are processed in accordance with Article 74 of the Firearms (Northern Ireland) Order 2004.  Each appeal can vary in complexity and the volume of information provided.  There are a number of factors which contribute to the processing times.  The PSNI’s Firearms & Explosive Branch provides the background information to an appeal which is usually provided within two to three months of the request, subject to available resources, and complex appeals may take longer.  The Department may also need to seek further details or updates from PSNI and/or health professionals.  On some occasions an appeal will be placed in abeyance pending the outcome of an investigation or court proceedings which may have a bearing on the appeal.  Appellants can also seek further time in which to provide further representations. The appeal team are not in a position to advise on how long the process will take, given that additional information may be required from external sources, which is determined on a case-by-case basis.  Cases are generally processed in date order, so processing times are also dependent on the number of live cases.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps he is taking to reduce waiting times for a first outpatient appointment for those suffering from neurological conditions, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: I acknowledge that the current waiting times for neurology are lengthy and growing for adults across Northern Ireland. This situation has arisen due to several factors, including funding that falls short of objectively assessed need and previous missed opportunities to reform health and social care delivery. I am committed to addressing this, subject to available funding.Each HSC Trust is responsible for prioritizing their neurology waiting lists and allocate resources accordingly, through the Elective Care Framework funding, Trusts will also have an opportunity to bid for funding specifically designated to address waiting lists. My Department’s regional review of neurology services sets the strategic direction to improve access to neurology services. There are a number of recommendations proposed in the final report, this includes growth in the workforce to provide additional capacity as well as an improved skill mix. This, in turn, will support new ways of working to ensure that access to outpatient appointments is prioritised for people who need to see a Consultant Neurologist, while also offering alternatives to outpatient appointments for those whose needs can be met in a different way.   A public consultation on the final report closed on 6th August 2025. My officials are currently considering the responses to that consultation which will inform the development of a final action plan. I expect this work to be completed in the coming months. In the interim, my officials continue to actively consider ways to address lengthy waiting lists and to understand how current systems could be effectively used to enhance patient access through new, and innovative ways of working and collaborative approaches. Tackling our lengthy waiting times is one of my priorities and is indeed an Executive priority.  It is, however, undoubtedly a long-term issue that requires sustainable and recurrent funding, workforce development and system wide transformation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps he is taking to reduce the four-year waiting list for neurology reviews in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: My Department is aware of the challenges faced by the Northern Trust and is committed to ensuring residents in the Northern Trust catchment have equity of access to neurology services.In addition, the final report of the Neurology Review proposes a number of recommendations which, if implemented, will have a direct impact on tackling waiting lists. The report’s recommendations, by creating additional capacity, improving the skill mix and optimising resources through referral management and the piloting of patient-initiated reviews, will support timely reviews and contribute to a reduction in waiting times and a realignment of capacity.In the interim, my officials continue to actively consider ways to address lengthy waiting lists and to understand how current systems could be effectively used to enhance patient access through new, and innovative ways of working and collaborative approaches. Tackling our lengthy waiting times is one of my priorities and is indeed an Executive priority.  It is, however, undoubtedly a long-term issue that requires sustainable and recurrent funding, workforce development and system wide transformation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on spend on active travel in Mid Ulster in the 2025-26 financial year.
answer_text: Funding for dedicated Active Travel projects is allocated by division rather than by constituency. Mid Ulster District Council falls within Western Division. I can confirm that £1m has been provided in opening budget allocations for 2025-26 towards dedicated active travel in this area and officials are currently developing a number of dedicated active travel schemes within the Mid Ulster District Council Area that are at various within the design process.Schemes for progression in the 2025/26 financial year include  the A29 Dungannon Road, Cookstown, Killyfaddy Road, Magherafelt, and active travel works associated with A45 Ballinakilly Road at Creenagh Bridge scheme.The A29 Cookstown Bypass will also provide opportunity for the provision of active travel infrastructure as part of the scheme plans.My Department also carries out many other activities for the benefit of pedestrians and cyclists that are captured under wider departmental active travel spend. Details of upcoming footway resurfacing and other pedestrian enhancements will be outlined in my Department’s annual report to Mid Ulster District Council.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether there has been an increase in convictions for drug-related offences in Mid Ulster over the last five years.
answer_text: ORAL QUESTION No 15 - AQO 2370/22-27		  ANSWER Figures for convictions held by the Department show that, for persons with an address in Mid Ulster constituency at the time of conviction, there were 69 convictions in 2020, 105 convictions in 2021, 97 convictions in 2022, 109 convictions in 2023 and 95 convictions in 2024.    Drugs offences may also be dealt with by way of out of court diversionary disposal, such as a caution, informed warning or Youth conference Program.  Figures held by the Department show that, for persons with an address in Mid ulster constituency at the time the diversion was implemented, there were 51 such cases in 2020, 51 in 2021, 42 in 2022, 53 in 2023 and 42 in 2024.  Given that figures for courts convictions for 2020 may have been impacted by the Covid pandemic, the figures in the years since do not suggest any significant increase in the number of convictions or diversionary disposals for drugs offences, for persons living in the Mid Ulster constituency. Note: 1. Figures for convictions relate to initial disposals at courts.  Appeals are not included. 2. Figures relate to cases where there was a conviction or an out of court diversionary disposal for at least one drugs offence. 3. Figures for diversions relate to cautions, informed warnings or Youth Conference Programs. 4. Figures relate to persons with an address in Mid Ulster Assembly constituency at the point they were dealt with at court or when the diversionary disposal was handed down.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline any steps he is taking to deal with the current delay in the Northern Ireland Environment Agency responding to planning application consultations.
answer_text: My officials are developing a Planning Improvement Plan in 2025/26. This plan will include a range of measures to improve planning performance and service and will cover 5 strands: 1.	Technical advice and guidance2.	Caseload management3.	Monitoring and reporting on performance4.	Process and IT systems  5.	Communication and stakeholder engagement.Current measures already being undertaken in addition to the Planning Improvement Plan, include:a review of staff resourcing and vacancy management as I recognise that this is one of the key areas in reducing the ongoing caseload levels. My department is therefore actively seeking to recruit additional staff for this work area.a review and update of internal processes, procedures and guidance including Standing Advice available on the DAERA website.analysis of consultations being receipted to inform targeted measures to reduce the level of re-consultation and improve the quality of information being submitted with a consultation. One development type being targeted through this is single dwelling consultations.engagement with other jurisdictions to see if any lessons can be learned from our counterparts in relation to how they operate within their planning systems.In addition, my department is working in collaboration with the DfI cross-governmental Planning Statutory Consultee Forum which is aiming to drive forward continuous improvements in the planning system.

To ask the Assembly Commission whether it is undertaking a review of security arrangements for guests and visitors to Parliament Buildings.
To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the number of cases where an individual found guilty of an offence relating to child sexual assault does not receive a custodial sentence.
answer_text: ANSWERProvisional figures for 2024 show that there were 105 convictions for sexual assaults on children. There was a non-custodial disposal in 44 of these cases – a rate of 41.9 per cent.Sentencing decisions are determined by the independent judiciary based on the individual circumstances for each case, which can vary widely from case-to-case, as well as any relevant sentencing guidance that is available, and the sentence maxima for the offence in question as specified in legislation. The sentencing exercise is a complex one and is unique to each case.   As I have said many times, it would be wholly inappropriate for me to comment on sentences imposed in individual cases; however, I am committed to ensuring that our children remain protected by the law from sexual predators.The comprehensive and robust legislative framework in place in Northern Ireland to deal with a wide range of sexual related behaviours, includes specific offences to target offending committed against under 13s, under 16s as well as under 18s.  For the most serious offences the available penalties are considerable.  In addition, to manage the risk of sexual harm going forward, those convicted are also placed on the sex offenders register and subject to notification requirements.  They can also be made the subject of a civil prevention order, such as the Sexual Offences Prevention Order which places tailored prohibitions and/or requirements on their behaviour.  Breach of the notification requirements or a SOPO, without reasonable excuse, is a criminal offence.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline what funding sources are available to victims and survivors groups from their office.
answer_text: TEO is the sponsor body for the Victims and Survivors Service (VSS) which provides funding to community groups to deliver essential support and services to victims and survivors of the Troubles/Conflict. In addition, VSS provides support directly to individuals through their Individual Needs Programme, including direct financial support through their self-directed assistance scheme. VSS is now engaged with our officials in preparation for a new Victims Support Programme from April 2026. This programme will allow support and services provided by VSS and its community partner organisations to continue. It will play a vital role in achieving the outcomes outlined within the Victims and Survivors Strategy. Funding will be subject to pre contract checks and demonstration of appropriate corporate governance arrangements.   The Ending Violence Against Women and Girl (EVAWG) Local Change Fund also provides funding to Voluntary and Community Groups for activity which meets the prevention outcomes in the EVAWG Strategic Framework.  Applications are made through Local Councils, however applications for 2025/26 are now closed and no decisions have been made for the 2026/27 year as yet. It is anticipated that further funding will be made available for the remainder of the 7-year strategy, based on an ongoing review of needs.The Central Good Relations Fund (CGRF) supports constituted voluntary and community sector groups to deliver good relations projects where there is evidence of identified good relations need.  Constituted Victims groups can apply to the fund, however addressing Good Relations issues should be at the heart of any project.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline what correspondence they have had with (i) other Executive Departments and (ii) local councils, regarding funding for victims and survivors groups.
answer_text: The Executive Office is the sponsor body for the Victims and Survivors Service (VSS) which provides funding to community groups to deliver essential support and services to victims and survivors of the Troubles/Conflict on a regional basis. As part of our commitment to supporting victims and survivors we have ensured that funding for VSS has consistently been protected. We have had no direct correspondence regarding funding to victims and survivors groups with other Executive Departments or local Councils.  However, as part of the Strategy for Victims and Survivors 2024-2034 public consultation, our officials engaged with Departments to raise awareness and understanding of the Strategy and issues relevant to each Department’s policies and operational areas.  Our officials will continue to engage with Departments throughout the Strategy implementation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline any support available to provide speed indicator devices at school locations.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department and I am committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and to address the needs of all road users.Speed Indicator Devices are normally provided by groups such as local Policing and Community Safety Partnerships who should be contacted for information on their future provision and support.My Department will continue to support Policing and Community Safety Partnerships to facilitate the safe installation of these devices. My Department’s focus at schools is on the provision of part-time 20mph speed limits which operate when pupils are arriving and leaving school.These installations are provided along with the necessary legislation to ensure that the speed limit is enforceable.Work is underway to develop a programme which will see the delivery of a third tranche of part-time 20s and I am planning to make an announcement on this is the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline any plans in place to provide financial support to community facilities in rural communities.
answer_text: My Department is continuing to provide support to rural communities through the Tackling Rural Poverty and Social Isolation (TRPSI) Framework. In the 2024/2025 financial year, over £7.8m of investment was delivered, across a range of initiatives. These included: contract funding and grant aid for the rural voluntary and community sector through the ongoing Rural Community Development Support Service provided by the Rural Support Networks; the Rural Micro Capital Grant Scheme; and, in conjunction with the Department for Communities, the Architectural Heritage Fund, the National Lottery Heritage Fund and the NIHE, the Village Catalyst Programme which is restoring historic buildings in community ownership and bringing them back to life to in turn address localised access to services, poverty and social isolation issues.  Grant aid for the rural business sector was provided through the Rural Business Development Grant Scheme; and support for the most vulnerable and marginalised continues through funding provided for the Farm Family Health Check Programme, and the Rural Support charity who continue to provide business and emotional mentoring and a range of other services for farmers and farm families.Grant aid to the Rural Support charity also funds the delivery of the Social Farming Support Service for Northern Ireland, through which over 100 vulnerable people benefit each week. I am very aware of how these targeted interventions make a significant impact in rural communities and therefore I am committed to ensuring further TRPSI schemes are delivered in 2025/2026 which will again assist with sustaining and enhancing community facilities. I hope to make some announcements on this matter in the coming weeks.Finally, my Department is also continuing to provide funding to rural communities through a range of other ongoing interventions including the inter-departmental Covid Recovery Small Settlements Regeneration Programme, City and Growth Deals, the Complementary Fund and PEACEPLUS.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any action he is taking to reduce occupational therapist assessment waiting times for (i) adults; and (ii) children in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Occupational therapy in adult and children’s services employ similar strategies to reduce occupational therapy waiting times within the limitations of existing resources, as outlined below:Targeting urgent clinical need via effective triage to prioritise those at greatest clinical risk.Pro-active waiting list management by implementation of a partial booking process to offer a choice of appointments in advance and reduce the numbers of appointments that are not attended.Regular waiting list validation and review of individual circumstances, should the service become aware of someone’s changing needs.Effective use of all available resources including timely and efficient recruitment processes to maintain funded staffing levels, deployment of staff to areas of greatest need and engagement of additional temporary staff / additional hours / overtime / peripatetic posts as available, to address service gaps.Pro-active absence management processes and support mechanisms to retain staff in work.Regular review of performance management to help ensure services working efficiently.Provision of both domiciliary assessment visits and clinic appointments as appropriate to the individual service user’s needs to ensure most efficient use of service capacity.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department will consider the inclusion of (i) Maghera Primary School; (ii) Donaghey Primary School; and (iii) Newmills Primary School in any future roll-out of the part-time 20mph speed limits at schools initiative.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department and I am committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and to address the needs of all road users. A review of the recent initiative introducing part-time 20mph speed limits at schools has been undertaken and is available on my Department’s internet site.  The review recommends the provision of a third tranche of part-time 20 mph speed limits at schools, however this will be more modest in size and is dependent on the availability of funding. The report also concludes that a more targeted approach is needed to ensure that the correct safety intervention is selected and work to update our policy document is underway.   I have asked officials to prepare the programme of schools to be treated in the next financial year based on the existing scored listing, but with preference given to rural schools which are located on roads where the national speed limit applies and obviously considering the safety of introducing such measures. Funding for progressing the next tranche of part-time 20 mph speed limits at schools is included within my budget plans for the next financial year.I would also like to see what can be done to improve attitudes to speeding, not only for road safety purposes, but to make places cleaner and greener.  I have therefore asked my officials to prepare a paper on the use of permanent 20mph speed limits particularly near schools, in predominately residential areas and places such as town centers where there is a higher number of those walking, wheeling and cycling.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether Inland Fisheries has determined that commercial trout fishing on Lough Neagh has no significant impacts on (i) the trout population of Lough Neagh; and (ii) the genetic integrity of Dollaghan in each tributary river, namely (a) the Blackwater River; (b) the Ballinderry River; (c) the Maine River; (d) the Moyola River; (e) the Six Mile Water; (f) the Upper Bann; (g) the Crumlin River; and (h) the Glenavy River.
answer_text: Commercial fishing for trout does remove fish from Lough Neagh, however conservation measures are in place to ensure that fish can spawn at least once when they reach maturity. Whilst netting survey data shows a decline on trout numbers in the lough, the trout status report shows that trout stocks in the tributaries flowing into Lough Neagh are stable or increasing. DAERA has not carried out any assessment of the impacts of commercial fishing on the genetic integrity of fish stocks on the individual tributaries listed.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the current time taken by the Northern Ireland Environment Agency to respond to planning consultations by local councils.
answer_text: As a statutory consultee within the planning system DAERA performance is measured against a duty to respond to a statutory consultation within 21 calendar days or otherwise agreed timescale.		Within this financial year, NIEA and Marine and Fisheries Division (MFD) have receipted 3,515 statutory planning consultations up to 31 January 2025. Across NIEA & MFD teams, 64% of statutory consultations were responded to within statutory timeframes (21 calendar days or otherwise agreed timescales). This is a dip of 8 percentage points in comparison to last year’s overall performance of 72%.  	Over the last few years, the Department has undertaken a range of measures to improve performance including: a review and update of internal processes, procedures and guidance; analysis of consultations being receipted to inform targeted measures to reduce the level of reconsultation and improve the quality of information being submitted with a consultation;engagement with a variety of stakeholders including specific sectoral engagements with agents to improve the quality of information submitted with a consultation ; andtemporary redeployment of additional caseworkersAdditional processes have been introduced such as the cessation of updates in order to allow staff to focus on the actual processing of consultations.   In addition, my Department is working in collaboration with the DfI cross-governmental Planning Statutory Consultee Forum and has contributed to a number of exercises aiming to drive forward continuous improvements in the planning system.But I accept that more is required and acknowledge that fluctuations in staffing capacity are one of the key issues impacting response rates. My Department is therefore actively seeking to recruit additional staff for this work area.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether changes or modifications to wastewater treatment plants are required to go through a Habitats Regulations Assessment to check the impact on designated sites such as Lough Neagh.
answer_text: The management of public wastewater infrastructure is an operational matter for NI Water. However, I have sought the requested information from NI Water and have been advised as follows:As part of the planning process, full Habitats Regulations Assessments (HRAs) are carried out by NI Water's Infrastructure Delivery Team, to determine the impact from the construction of, or modification to, wastewater treatment plants on the designated site and to identify mitigation measures for inclusion in the HRA, to protect any identified designations within the vicinity of the construction site.In addition, in advance of NI Water applying to the Northern Ireland Environment Agency (NIEA) for a consent to discharge, the company undertakes a high-level assessment, known as a hydrological trace, to determine if the proposed discharge is to a watercourse which flows into a designated waterbody.  Once complete, this assessment is provided to NIEA to be taken into consideration as part of the application for a consent to discharge.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.comMaking direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

Prior to this date, there were a number of known issues regarding the physical condition of the building and, because of its designation as a Hearing Centre, it was open one day per week, primarily for Magistrates Court business.In December 2023, the Northern Ireland Courts and Tribunals Service (NICTS) published its Estate Strategy, Making the Place for Justice.https://www.justice-ni.gov.uk/publications/northern-ireland-courts-and-tribunals-service-estate-strategy-2023-2030The Strategy is structured around 4 Improvement Aims and 12 Strategic Outcomes that, if realised, will result in a courts and tribunals estate that is capable of providing modernised and improved facilities for all citizens of Northern Ireland.To implement its Estate Strategy, NICTS will use data and evidence to baseline the existing estate against the agreed Strategic Outcomes, and develop prioritised investment delivery plans in the context of the finite resources available.	Decisions on the future use of Magherafelt Courthouse will form part of this process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline what funding is available from her Department for restorative justice programmes.
answer_text: The work of the Youth Justice Agency over the past 20 years has been underpinned by restorative justice.  In recognition of the effectiveness of restorative approaches to deal with the consequences of offending, my Department is extending this work into the adult justice system.Aside from annual Youth Justice Agency funding, my Department provides a small amount of grant-in-aid funding to two community-based schemes currently accredited to deliver restorative justice locally.  These are Northern Ireland Alternatives and Community Restorative Justice Ireland, who each received £69,000 this year.  This is legacy funding dating back to accreditation under the 2007 Government Protocol.  It is provided to the head offices of each organisation for core salaries and running costs aimed at meeting Departmental objectives in relation to restorative justice delivery.  Other organisations may be in receipt of funding from my Department for a range of work, some of which may be based on restorative principles, but this is the only formal funding that my Department currently provides for the delivery of restorative justice.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any plans he has to release another tranche for the Rural Micro Grants Scheme 2024/2025 during this financial year.
answer_text: The first tranche of the 2024/25 Rural Micro Capital Grant Scheme opened for applications on 14 June 2024 and Letters of Offer to the value of £1.502m issued to 916 rural voluntary and community organisations on 31 August 2024.The claims for this first tranche are currently being processed and I anticipate making a decision on a further tranche of the 2024/25 scheme later this month.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any plans he has to provide further support to communities through the Rural Halls Refurbishment Scheme.
answer_text: The Rural Halls Refurbishment Scheme was a Rural Policy Framework pilot which provided grants to rural community and voluntary sector organisations to deliver capital works to rural halls, with the aim of keeping community facilities viable and sustainable. Under the scheme, a total of £3.89m was awarded to 104 voluntary and community organisations between 2021/22 and 2023/2024. In April 2024, I established a Rural Policy Unit which is focused on developing for consultation, a new evidence-based rural policy. As part of this work, previous rural policy initiatives and interventions, including the Rural Halls Refurbishment Scheme, will be reviewed and evaluated. In due course, my officials will be engaging with other departments, councils, public authorities and stakeholders to inform these collaborative future rural policy proposals for consultation.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the current policy in relation to the leashing of dogs in his Department's parks and forestry facilities.
answer_text: Dogs must be kept under control and restrained from giving reasonable cause for annoyance or alarm to other persons or animals in all DAERA parks and forests. The anticipated result of the delivery of the Action Plan being a reduction in the risks posed by damp and mould to both customers, properties and organisation through effective communication, education, timely and effective response maintenance and a more targeted planned scheme works programme. Some of the key actions taken to date are:•	Cyclical assurance visits to properties with more than one reported case of Damp, Mould or Condensation in the previous 2 years•	A commitment to carry out inspections at properties with a report of Damp, Mould or Condensation within 24 hours•	A significant social media campaign aimed at customers on how to reduce, resolve and report Damp, Mould or Condensation•	Planned training for over 1000 staff in a range of different job roles, including technical staff and non-technical frontline staff.•	Promotion of our message to staff of “Don’t Walk By” to ensure any signs of Damp, Mould or Condensation in our properties are addressed.The issue of Damp, Mould or Condensation is a corporate priority for the organisation and extensive work is both ongoing and planned to deliver for customers.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Housing Executive directly. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of children awaiting percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy insertion surgery; and (ii) the current waiting time for the surgery, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The issue of access to percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy (PEG) insertion for children and young people is an important issue as is the range of all surgical procedures that face children and young people in Northern Ireland.  This procedure is a complex procedure that requires specialised clinical and nursing input prior to, during and post procedure.  There are 93 children currently on the waiting list.  As this procedure is carried out only at the RBHSC all 93 children on the list are listed as BHSCT patients.  Due to the introduction of the new ICT system, Encompass, in BHSCT it is not possible to examine the data further at this stage. It is hoped that when the Encompass system is fully operational across all Trusts in NI that the extraction and publication of data will be more streamlined.Each case is constantly assessed by the specialist team at the RBHSC and dealt with as quickly as possible.  The Trust is currently examining means to address this issue and it is hoped that this can be put in place from October.  A Paediatric Surgeon is currently going through an urgent validation of Peg Tube waiting lists.  This process will identity which children are suitable to transfer to Alderhey Hospital in Liverpool for the procedure.  Clinical criteria, agreed by the specialist team, will be in place to determine this.Belfast Trust are also hoping to provide a waiting list initiative in October, to carry out additional sessions on Fridays, with patients being nursed in Knox ward in RBHSC over the weekend. Further discussions are underway with South Eastern Trust to provide the Belfast Trust paediatric surgeon a gastrostomy surgery list in the Ulster Hospital.  Belfast Trust Gastrostomy Specialist Nurses will provide training to staff in South Eastern Trust to help facilitate this.A further review of surgical work performed in RBHSC is underway and any work that can be redirected to District General Hospitals will be transferred.  This may free up capacity in RBHSC for Paediatric Surgeons to perform more gastrostomy surgery and other more complex surgery that can only be carried out in RBHSC.The Trust are also examining partnership working with Private Sector to use RBHSC capacity over the weekend to deliver Waiting List Initiatives in the Children’s Hospital and will be dependent on unscheduled care pressures. My Officials will continue to meet with BHSCT to discuss these potential solutions to examine their progress and success.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on the current funding provision for the PSNI.
answer_text: ORAL QUESTION No 14 - AQO 750/22-27		ANSWERPSNI’s opening Non-ringfenced Resource budget for 2024-25 was £807.7m including £31.2m of Additional Security Funding. However, I remain committed to working with the Chief Constable, the Minister of Finance and my Executive colleagues to ensure that we have a Police Service that is funded appropriately.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what strategy he has in place to maintain or increase the total annual megawatt output generated by (i) onshore wind; and (ii) solar energy by 2030.
answer_text: Given the close relationship between the three questions from Mr Keith Buchanan, the answers have been included as one response below.The Executive’s Energy Strategy with associated annual action plans, being delivered in the context of the 2022 Climate Change Act, sets out the strategy to deliver at least 80% of electricity consumption from renewable sources including onshore wind, solar and other technologies by 2030. The oversight and governance of the strategy considers obstacles and mitigation measures to delivering the target and are reflected in the actions published annually.The 2024 action plan is published on the Department for the Economy website.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the forecasted annual megawatt output which will be generated by (i) onshore wind; and (ii) solar energy, up until 2030, broken down by each method of generation.
answer_text: The Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 established a target of 80% of electricity consumption to be from renewable sources by 2030. Essential to meeting this target will be the establishment of a support scheme for renewable electricity. Departmental officials are currently working on a scheme design that will incentivise investment in larger scale renewable electricity projects ensuring consumers pay a fair price for electricity produced locally.The expected volumes of supported renewable generation are based on the 2023 Generation and Capacity Statement published by The System Operator For Northern Ireland (SONI), who forecasted the electricity demand of the north in 2030 to be between 8.9 and 11.3 Terawatt-hour (TWh). The High-Level design for a renewable electricity support scheme, published in April this year, has estimated that between 4 and 6 Terawatt-hour (TWh) of additional renewable electricity generation will be required in order to meet the 2030 target. A breakdown by each method of generation has not been forecasted by the Department at this early stage of the renewable electricity support scheme development. SONI, as the Transmission System Operator (TSO), and Northern Ireland Electricity Networks (NIE Networks) as Distribution System Operator (DSO) are the most suitable sources for up to date forecasts.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of the current waiting time between when a date of need is established and the application for funding is submitted for chair lifts in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) what action he will take to reduce the waiting time.
answer_text: i. The regional ministerial (PfA) target for installation of home-lifts (stairlifts, inter-floor lifts, platform lifts etc.) is 16 weeks from the date the need is confirmed.  The waiting time from the date the need for a home lift is established to the application for funding is submitted, varies depending on clinical need and the budgetary position.  Management of the annual funding allocation requires a stringent scrutiny process to ensure the monthly allocation of funding to approve new orders remains within the budgetary allocation.  Inevitably this will cause delays in approving orders, to remain within budget.  Home-lift approvals are prioritised based on clinical need and risk.  Urgent provision for items required for example in an end of life scenario, are approved immediately.  Actual installation time will then depend on supplier /manufacturer capacity.ii. Waiting times for home lifts as indicated is dependent on the budget status at any point in time against the prevailing demand.  Assurances around adequate available funding to meet all demand would be required, to eliminate wait times for home lifts.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether vehicles that are taxed, insured and have received a Temporary Exemption Certificate can be driven in (i) Scotland; (ii) England; (iii) Wales; (iv) Republic of Ireland; and (v) European countries.
answer_text: Private cars first registered between 1 June 2019 and 31 May 2020 or between 1 June 2017 and 31 May 2018, which have a valid MOT issued by the Driver and Vehicle Agency will have a one-year Temporary Exemption Certificate (TEC) automatically applied.When a TEC has been applied, motorists can continue to drive their car legally, if it is kept in a roadworthy condition, is taxed and insured. This includes driving in Britain, the South and European countries.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the action she is taking to recover unpaid rates.
answer_text: Land & Property Services has robust billing, collection and recovery processes in place to maximise rate collection and reduce rating debt. These processes include issuing annual rate bills and final demands for payment. Where payment, or arrangements to pay, are not made, they will initiate recovery procedures. This includes formal written correspondence, e-mails and direct telephone contact with ratepayers. Land & Property Services will progress legal recovery actions for persistent non-payment. This includes progression through the Magistrate Courts, the Enforcement of Judgement’s Office and initiation of bankruptcy proceedings. Ratepayers experiencing financial difficulties are encouraged to contact Land & Property Services to discuss payment options which can include restructuring current payment plans or establishing longer term payment arrangements to enable payment of rate arrears.

To ask the Assembly Commission for an update on the roof repairs to Parliament Buildings.
To ask the Minister for Communities to outline what financial support will be available for community halls, following the Community Halls Pilot Programme in 2017.
answer_text: The Community Halls Capital Grant Scheme was delivered during the 2016/17 financial year. The Department made grants of up to £25,000 available towards Community Halls Minor Works. Approximately £1.9m was allocated to enhance 89 facilities. My Departments capital budget allocation for 2024/25 is significantly reduced from previous years. All capital funding has now been allocated which has resulted in significant impacts across all sectors including housing, and the Culture, Arts, Heritage, and Sports sectors.  I recognise the positive impact the pilot programme had on organisations, enabling them to enhance their facilities and providing support which helped sustain the vital activities they deliver within local communities. At present there are no plans for the launch of a new scheme however I will continue to monitor the capital position and will liaise with the sector if it is possible to provide support in the future.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline his Department’s programme for grass cutting to maintain visibility and road safety.
answer_text: Grass cutting operations are carried out by my Department specifically for road safety reasons and not for cosmetic or amenity purposes. 	It would be my intention to deliver a programme of grass cutting in accordance with the new approach to verge management across the road network that I announced in October 2022.  	The new approach increases focus on protecting wildlife and promoting biodiversity, without compromising on roads safety and will support the actions needed to comply with the Climate Change Act passed by the Assembly in 2022.	A single swathe only, typically not less than 1.0m, will be cut along the verges on the strategic road network twice per year and, where possible, verges on lighter trafficked rural roads will be cut once per year, towards the end of the growing season.	However, areas that are needed for road safety purposes, such as for sight lines at junctions, will be cut at least twice each year to enable road users to see on-coming traffic when exiting the junction. 	Other areas of grass and vegetation that would compromise forward visibility, such as at bends, can also be cut at least twice each year to enable road users to see oncoming traffic or other hazards.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline what measures he will take to improve respite services for carers in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Short Break provision for children with disability within the Northern Health and Social Care Trust may be delivered by the Trust or via an organisation contracted by the Trust. There are three Short Break Units for children with disability in the Northern Trust area (Whitehaven Short Break Unit, Edenview Short Break Unit & The Ferns). Currently, there are regional pressures that exist with Short Break Provision (particularly overnight residential short break provision). All the HSC Trusts are working with my Department to develop potential options to address the increasing regional demand, noting the very challenging financial environment. The Northern Trust has had to temporarily re-purpose Edenview Short Break Unit to provide longer term placements for children, however, it is anticipated that the Trust will revert back to providing Short Breaks within Edenview late Summer 2024, when alternative permanent placements are hopefully confirmed for this cohort of children. The Northern Trust continue to develop their Sharing The Care Short Break Service to meet changing need, and ensure a higher Short Break provision across the Trust area. My Department is also currently finalising the Children With Disability Framework. It is recognised that a review and reform of how services are structured, delivered and resourced, both from a regional perspective and locally in each of the Trusts is required. This includes the provision of Short Breaks.A Children With Disability Workstream has also been established as part of the Children’s Social Care Services Strategic Reform Board.  This workstream will be looking specifically at the regional and local position in respect of Children With Disability, and will include a focus on short breaks. The outcomes of this work will inform future commissioning.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department will increase the number of funded nursery places in Mid Ulster for 2024-25.
answer_text: The Education Authority Pre-School Education Group is responsible for managing the Pre-School Education Programme and is tasked with ensuring there is sufficient pre-school provision in all areas to meet the commitment to provide a funded pre-school education place for every target age child whose parents want it.Stage One of the pre-school admissions process concluded on 25 April with 98.33% of children offered a funded pre-school place in a setting of their parents’ preference.  Stage Two of the admissions process is currently underway and parents will be advised of the outcome of their applications on 11 June.The EA has confirmed that sufficient funded pre-school places remain in all areas with 76 funded places still available in the Mid Ulster Parliamentary Constituency area at close of Stage One to accommodate 12 children who submitted further preferences at Stage Two of the admissions process as they were yet to be offered a place in a setting of their parents’ preference.  The EA has advised therefore there are no plans to increase the levels of pre-school provision in Mid Ulster at this time.  The EA PEG will, however, continue to monitor provision in all areas.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline the amount of funding that has been allocated to each victims and survivors group in Mid Ulster.
answer_text: Mid Ulster Victims Empowerment (MUVE) is the only victims’ and survivors’ group based specifically in Mid Ulster that receives funding. It has received just over two hundred and three thousand pounds over the last three years. However, many funded organisations including, amongst others, Relatives for Justice and the South East Fermanagh Foundation, provide support outside of the area in which their headquarters are based, through satellite offices, outreach and online engagement.  These and other funded groups operate across all counties and constituencies which provide support to victims and survivors living in all areas here.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many hernia repair operations have been carried out in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust, in the last twelve months.
answer_text: The various waiting times for hernia repair operations depend on several factors. These include the type of hernia a patient may have, the severity of their condition, as well as their personal circumstances. My officials have been advised that 576 hernia repair operations have been carried out in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust, in the last twelve months. Given the reduced waiting list initiative funding which has been allocated in Quarter 1, Trusts have had to prioritise this funding for red flag and absolute time critical patients. Within my previous request for funding in the recent Budget exercise to deliver additional waiting list initiatives, it had been my Department’s intention to target £4m this year at supporting approximately 800 of our hernia patients who have been waiting for the longest period time. Unfortunately the Budget allocation provided no targeted resource.
