question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether his Department plans to introduce grants for the installation of green energy alternatives, such as anaerobic digestion, in the 2024-25 financial year.
answer_text: Further to my earlier response to AQW 10022/22-27, my Department is working on the following:Biomethane Policy DevelopmentA Call for Evidence on developing a sustainable biomethane sector in this region is due to issue shortly.  A key step in the development of an effective policy framework for biomethane, the Call for Evidence will seek information and views on a range of issues, including those affecting the economic viability of local biomethane production via anaerobic digestion and possible support mechanisms.  While responses will help to inform the best way forward, any options identified for supporting the sector will require further development and will not be introduced during the current financial year.   Renewable Electricity Support Scheme  The Department is developing a renewable electricity support scheme to incentivise investment, ensure a fair price for electricity produced locally and encourage diversification to support security of supply.  Following our recent consultation, officials have been working with consultants to define the optimal scheme structure. The High-Level Design for the scheme was published this month as part of the Department’s response to the consultation.  The next phase will involve modelling and a financial impact assessment alongside the establishment of a legislative pathway, state aid approval and institutional roles and responsibilities that are necessary for delivery.  Final arrangements and considerations of eligibility into the scheme for each technology type, including anaerobic digestion, will be incorporated in the Detailed Design to be developed throughout the remainder of 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether his Department plans to introduce grants for the installation of green energy alternatives in the 2024-25 financial year.
answer_text: My department is working in partnership with Invest NI to deliver a new scaled phase of its Energy & Resource Efficiency (ERE) Programme for Business. The Programme will include a new Energy Efficiency Capital Grant Scheme (EECG) which will be open to the wider business base and is due to launch in the coming weeks. The EECG will include grant support for energy efficiency measures along with low carbon and renewable technologies, all aimed at reducing emissions and helping businesses take control of their energy use by lowering their energy demand. The wider aspects of the ERE Programme include support in the form of Technical Consultancy, Resource Matching Through Industrial Symbiosis and a Resource Efficiency Capital Grant which are already in operation.  My department is also working in partnership with the Strategic Investment Board (SIB) in the delivery of the ‘Energy Invest to Save’ fund. A call for applications as been made across Government Departments and Arm’s Length Bodies for 2024/25. This targeted funding offers 100% support for Government Departments to deliver energy capital projects. Successful projects to date have covered areas such as energy efficiency, renewable energy, energy storage, EV charging infrastructure and automatic metering resulting in significant energy, costs, and carbon reductions in the government estate.   My department is currently preparing a consultation on evidence-based options for a domestic energy efficiency programme in line with Action 7 of the Energy Strategy Action Plan for 2024. The outcome of this consultation will help to inform options regarding the design, rules, and guidance of future support.  In the interim, the department is working with partners such as the Utility Regulator to assess the options for further scaling of up existing energy efficiency programmes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of whether NI Water is effectively discharging its functions to ensure the efficient delivery of housing.
answer_text: NI Water’s status as a regulated utility company means that the Utility Regulator protects the interests of consumers by challenging NI Water to deliver high quality, value for money water and sewerage services.  NI Water is encouraging early engagement with developers, through the Pre- Development Enquiry process, to arrange for initial discussions on wastewater issues at the beginning of the process to try and find a resolution to any issues at an early stage.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what plans he has to provide funding support for households to install heat pumps, similar to those available in other regions of the United Kingdom.
answer_text: My department is currently preparing a public consultation on a low carbon heat support scheme in line with Action 8 of the Energy Strategy Action Plan for 2023. The outcome of this consultation will help to inform options regarding the design, rules, and guidance of future support for low carbon heating technologies which will include heat pumps.https://www.economy-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/economy/Energy-Strategy-2023-Action-Plan-Report.pdf

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the reason for the reduction in Public Health Agency funding to Mid Ulster District Council for the Agewell scheme.
answer_text: The Mid Ulster Agewell Project is part-funded by the Public Health Agency (PHA). The other funding partners are the Northern Health and Social Care Trust (NHSCT), the Southern Health and Social Care Trust (SHSCT), the NI Housing Executive (NIHE), the local Policing and Community Safety Partnership (PSCP) and Mid-Ulster District Council (MUDC). The PHA has informed me that funding for the Mid Ulster Agewell Project was agreed across all partners, with the requirement that the Agewell Partnership would source additional external funding to make the project sustainable in the longer term.The PHA needs to ensure an equitable split of investment for Later Years projects across all Northern Area Councils. Given this, it was agreed at the initiation of the Agewell Project there would be stepped reduction in PHA funding over the past four years.The stepped reduction also allowed for additional PHA investment in an Age Friendly Coordinator for the area, and this was agreed with MUDC in 2020. The implementation of Age Friendly was the result of a regional business case and commissioning agreement with all eleven Council Areas in NI.The PHA recently received notification from MUDC that, after a successful tendering process, they have awarded the contract for 24/25 to the Mid-Ulster Agewell Partnership (who have held the contract to-date). The Mid Ulster Agewell Project will be delivered in 2024/25 with a revised specification and Key Performance Indicators (KPIs).

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the maximum (a) capacity; and (b) output when MOT test centres are operating at 100 per cent; (ii) the number of staff required to achieve the 100 per cent output; and (iii) the current staffing compliment at test centres, broken down by test centre.
answer_text: The Driver & Vehicle Agency (DVA) currently has 15 operational vehicle test centres geographically spread across the North.  Those test centres have a total of 46 light lanes capable of testing private cars, light goods vehicles, taxis and motorcycles.  During 2022-2023, the DVA conducted approximately 1.09 million vehicle tests on light lanes, which does not include vehicles that failed to attend booked appointments.There are two teams in each test centre working an Extended Day Working (EDW) pattern from 8:00 to 20:30, Monday to Saturday.  They work on a three days on, three days off shift pattern.  The 46 light lanes can accommodate a maximum of 137 vehicle examiners per shift.  Voluntary overtime on Sundays and bank holidays is not part of the normal shift pattern.    Taking into account annual leave, bank holidays, special leave, sickness absence, training, reasonable adjustments, phased return, personalised hours, team briefings, maintenance and calibration of equipment etc; the maximum number of days available for each vehicle examiner on the EDW pattern is on average 110.5 days per year.Based on the above, the maximum vehicle testing capacity for private cars, light goods vehicles, taxis and motorcycles is approximately 930,000 vehicles per year, which does not include tests conducted on Sundays and bank holidays.The staffing complement of light lane vehicle examiners broken down by test centre, based on 43 test lanes with three staff each and three test lanes with two staff each is set out in Table 1 below:Table 1EDW A EDW B Total Armagh6612Ballymena9918Balmoral121224Coleraine9918Cookstown9918Craigavon 9918Downpatrick6612Enniskillen6612Larne6612Lisburn111122Mallusk9918Newry9918Newbuildings121224Newtownards 181836Omagh6612Total137137274The current staffing complement of light lane vehicle examiners, which includes over-staffing, broken down by test centre is set out in Table 2 below:Table 2EDW A EDW B Standard DayTotal Armagh6713Ballymena111324Balmoral15131.229.2Coleraine101121Cookstown101121Craigavon 9716Downpatrick5611Enniskillen460.610.6Larne570.612.6Lisburn1080.618.6Mallusk151429Newry101020Newbuildings15101.226.2Newtownards 17160.633.6Omagh56111471454.86296.8

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) to detail the current policy in relation to Access NI checks in schools; and (ii) how frequently they have to be renewed for an existing employee.
answer_text: Staff working in grant-aided schools are employed by individual school Boards of Governors / employing authorities and not by the Department of Education.  All education workers employed in a statutory education setting in a regulated activity post are required to complete an enhanced Access NI check before they begin work.The current policy in relation to AccessNI checks in schools is set out in DE Circular 2013/1, The policy does not require checks to be renewed for existing employees in grant-aided schools unless there is a break in service of three months or more.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to provide an update on the (i) capital works for strengthening Ardtrea bridge, Moneymore; and (ii) whether it will be included in the budget for 2024-25.
answer_text: The design work for the replacement of the Ardtrea Bridge is largely complete and my officials are continuing to progress the scheme with commencement of the construction phase planned for the 2024/25 financial year, subject to the availability of funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how his Department calculates allowances for fostering families in comparison with the rest of the United Kingdom.
answer_text: The Department reviews the allowances payable to foster carers on an annual basis in respect of the decision for an annual uplift to apply to these allowances. The annual review considers rates of inflation, the rates of allowances payable in other UK jurisdictions, and what is affordable within the financial envelope available to the Department. It is regrettable that, due to budget pressures, the allowance rates payable to foster carers in Northern Ireland have now fallen behind the minimum allowance rates payable in other parts of the UK. However to note, the allowance rates in NI and those in other parts of the UK may not be directly comparable, as the age bandings for rates (allowances) are not all co-terminus, and there may also be other differences in the amount of allowances payable and the costs which the minimum allowances are intended to cover.Current rates for 2023-24 are available on the Department’s website at Foster care allowances update | Department of Health (health-ni.gov.uk). A decision on the rates payable for 2024-25, will be undertaken as soon as the budget for 2024-25 is known and foster carers will be notified of the outcome as soon as possible. Payment of any uplifts will be backdated to April 2024.The Fostering allowance includes provision for the following:Food (including school meals)	40%Household costs			25%Clothing and footwear		18%Pocket money			10%Travel costs				7%The model scheme allows for some flexibility regarding the spending of these allowances as set out in Part 1 of the scheme and foster carers are free to spend food, household and travel allowances as they feel benefits the child most.  Separate to this standard model scheme allowance, other specific support is available for items such as disposable nappies, school uniforms and university expenses.  Mileage can also be claimed.  The Independent Review of Children’s Social Care Services in Northern Ireland, published in June 2023, identified payments to foster carers as a priority area for investment. Recognising the central role of foster carers as part of the team around the child, a dedicated workstream has been established as part of the Children’s Social Care Strategic Reform Programme, focused on foster carer recruitment and retention. This workstream will include consideration of the rates of allowances payable to foster carers and the support available to help them in the vital work they do.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether breast reduction surgery is available to those who have an increased risk of breast cancer.
answer_text: Where appropriate patients with familial / genetic risk will be offered risk reduction surgery in all Trusts. All trusts provide breast risk reduction surgery (i.e. mastectomy rather than breast reduction surgery) to reduce the risk of breast cancer.During the COVID pandemic there was a moratorium on risk reduction breast surgery. Access to surgery was restricted and only emergency, urgent and cancer surgeries were prioritised. Trusts are rebuilding capacity and these procedures have recommenced. However, given current pressures patients are clinically prioritised for surgery meaning those patients with a confirmed breast cancer are listed for surgery first. Teams are very conscious of the needs of this patient group and actively manage all capacity in order to try to provide access to surgery in as timely a way as possible.Reducing waiting times for elective care is a priority across the Northern Ireland Health Service and strenuous efforts are being made across the HSC, including rebuilding core services back to pre-Covid levels and beyond.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether bilateral mastectomy is available in each Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: All trusts provide bilateral mastectomy where clinically indicated to remove cancer or to reduce the risk of breast cancer.During the COVID pandemic there was a moratorium on risk reduction breast surgery. Access to surgery was restricted and only emergency, urgent and cancer surgeries were prioritised. Trusts are rebuilding capacity and these procedures have recommenced. However, given current pressures patients are clinically prioritised for surgery meaning those patients with a confirmed breast cancer are listed for surgery first.  Teams are very conscious of the needs of this patient group and actively manage all capacity in order to try to provide access to surgery in as timely a way as possible.Reducing waiting times for elective care is a priority across the Northern Ireland Health Service and strenuous efforts are being made across the HSC.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will review the baseline funding payable to community pharmacies.
To ask the Minister of Health (i) to outline the current accessibility to Kuvan; (ii) when this was last reviewed; and (iii) when it will be next reviewed.
answer_text: Kuvan (also known as sapropterin) has been routinely available in Northern Ireland (NI) for treating hyperphenylalanaemia in people aged up to 21 years with phenylketonuria, and pregnant women, since October 2021 in line with the recommendations made in NICE Technical Appraisal (TA) 729.Subsequent to publication of NICE TA 729, the availability of sapropterin has been extended to eligible patients of all ages in England. This extension of use was based on a lower price being agreed by NHS England out with the NICE arrangements for access to a generic version of the medicine which is now available.In December 2023 my Department’s Permanent Secretary made a bespoke public interest decision under the Executive Formation Act to approve a regional position for the commissioning of generic sapropterin for patients of all ages. This decision was made on the basis that the acquisition cost of generic sapropterin available to the HSC is equivalent to the NHS England price and so treatment at this price is therefore deemed to be cost-effective by NICE.My Department’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) issued a Service Notification to HSC Trusts on 2 February 2024 setting out the expectations for implementation of this decision, in line with Circular HSC (SQSD) 12/22. It is recognised that the clinical pathway for assessment is complex, requiring intensive clinical oversight and monitoring. A phased approach to implementation is being taken forward to develop a clinical pathway for access, based on the UK best practice consensus pathway produced by the British Inherited Metabolic Diseases Group, with specific and immediate provision to ensure that no patient already receiving treatment is forced to cease treatment upon reaching their 22nd birthday.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what plans are in place in increase the number of orthopedic surgeries to alleviate waiting times.
answer_text: Reducing waiting times across elective care, including orthopaedics, is a key priority for me. Current waiting times are unacceptable, no one should have to wait longer than necessary for the surgery they need.  While good progress has been made in tackling waiting times in some areas, there remains much more to be done. Within the constraints of existing budgets, I’m committed to taking whatever actions I can to tackle these waiting times and ensure that patients are treated as quickly as possible to ensure best possible outcomes.In April 2022 my Department commissioned an independent external review of the regional elective orthopaedic service in Northern Ireland to identify proposals for the immediate recovery of the service. Since the completion of the review significant progress has been made across the region, with some Trusts now exceeding pre-Covid levels of activity.  For example, in Musgrave Park Hospital, more than 1,700 additional patients, over and above activity planned prior to the review, have been treated to date and the newly refurbished Duke of Connaught Unit at the Hospital is now delivering high volume, low complexity treatments for patients with over 1,700 procedures and 750 outpatient appointments delivered in the unit thus far. However, I accept that there is more to do and providing further improvement in orthopaedic waiting lists is important to improve the health outcomes of our population.I recognise that the transformation of HSC services alone is not enough to remove our unacceptably long waiting times. While it will help bridge the existing capacity / demand gap and in some specialities/procedures, may also reduce waiting lists, more needs to be done. Going forward, we therefore need to invest in additionality to reduce waits. Any future funding allocation to help reduce waiting lists in orthopaedics will however need to be considered as part of a broader review of resource investment in Waiting List Initiatives.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what action is being taken to help people in emergency mental health and addiction crisis.
answer_text: Implementing the new regional crisis service is a commitment in the Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031, under actions 12 and 27.  The Strategic Planning and Performance Group and the Public Health Agency are working in partnership to deliver on both of these actions, to develop a Regional Mental Health Crisis Service for NI. The components of the regional mental health crisis service which have been taken forward are:  accessing mental health crisis services, primary care and interagency partnership, community crisis service, mental health liaison service and crisis resolution home treatment. A mapping report of current crisis services including community and voluntary sector is currently been finalised. Phase 2 future state work has commenced including scoping what a good service would look like, what are the primary drivers for change and the proposed change ideas. A Regional Mental Health Crisis Service workshop is taking place in March 2024 with all relevant stakeholders including substance use representatives to progress this work and to improve pathways and access to emergency mental health crisis. Furthermore, the significant link between substance use and mental health has been  highlighted in the Substance Use Strategy and within the priority actions of the Substance Use Strategic Plan. These actions will strengthen links between substance use and mental health services, particularly in preventative, crisis, treatment and recovery services as detailed in the Mental Health Strategy. The Substance Use Strategic Plan includes priority actions around the need for a whole system approach to developing person-centred pathways. These pathways will make sure that people receive the right services at the right time. The Plan also prioritises the development of a workforce that is skilled in brief interventions in substance use, and in the review and reconfiguration of substance use liaison services for people who are using substances, when they come into contact with mental health inpatient services.In line with the usual process for commissioning of services, any decision to commission mental health crisis services in the future will be based on an assessment of the level of demand for services, and within the context of future budget availability.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the work being undertaken to increase the provision of, and early access to, mental health services for (i) adults; and (ii) children, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The following work is being undertaken to increase the provision of and early access to mental health services for adults this information is broken down by Health and Social Care Trust  All Trusts are experiencing significant pressures as a consequence of increased demands upon services, including increased numbers of new referrals, increased complexity and acuity of people presenting to the service and significant workforce pressures as the result of increased vacancy levels.Belfast Health and Social Care Trust Within the Belfast Trust Community Mental Health staff are engaged in outpatient modernisation work to create capacity in Community Mental Health Teams through the use of professional initiated pathway for patients who are currently stable and not requiring active tertiary level intervention.  The objective of this work is to reduce review clinics and create capacity for new patient appointments and facilitate speedy re-entry into treatment without the need for GP referral.Acute inpatient services continue to work diligently to reduce delayed discharges, to reduce unnecessary bed days thus creating swift admission to inpatient treatment at the point of need.The above are projects that will continue in the absence of additional funding.Northern Health & Social Care TrustWithin Northern Trust, early intervention to people with Mental Health needs is most frequently provided through Community Mental Health Services and Crisis Resolution Home Treatment Services.  Northern Trust Community Mental Health Team’s triage all referrals received on a daily basis, urgent referrals are seen within 5 days, and routine referrals within 9 weeks.  Community Mental Health Teams collaborate with services across the local community as appropriate to promote early intervention and to enable recovery.  Community Mental Health Teams maintain close working relationships with primary care colleagues and mental health staff within the GP federation. The Crisis Resolution Home Treatment Team operates a 24 hr/7day multidisciplinary mental health service available to adults who are experiencing an acute deterioration in their mental health. The Crisis Resolution service seeks to safely manage the immediate needs of service users not currently known to services or those known to community mental health services who require support outside normal working hours. In addition, the Mental Health Liaison Service provides a comprehensive mental health assessment and intervention to service users referred from acute hospital wards and service users who present to the Emergency Department across both Antrim Area and Causeway Hospital sites. Individuals are directed to attend Emergency Departments usually by their GP where there is a requirement for assessment of physical health and mental health care needs. In the absence of a physical health care need, individuals experiencing an acute deterioration in their mental health are directed to Crisis Resolution Home Treatment T

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what plans are in place to increase the provision of domiciliary care packages in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: In common with all parts of the region, the Northern Health and Social Trust is experiencing challenges in securing capacity to meet increasing demand for domiciliary care.  The Trust has reviewed the systems within their in-house Homecare service to improve efficiency and deliver higher levels of face-to-face time with service users. The Northern Trust have contracts with 22 Independent Sector Providers which includes 3 new providers who signed contracts in the last year.  The Trust has commenced a contract with a new provider in the Mid-Ulster area and anticipates it will increase capacity in this locality. My Department continues to work to maximise capacity in social care and to engage with service providers in addressing the ongoing issues of recruitment and retention to ensure a skilled and dedicated domiciliary care workforce. As you may be aware, following the publication of an Evaluation and Summary Report on the Reform of Adult Social Care Consultation, in May 2023, the Department established the Social Care Collaborative Forum (SCCF) to commence the delivery of reforms, with a number of workstreams established across a range of key issues. SCCF workstreams are developing a plan to grow capacity in domiciliary care across the region, a new model for domiciliary care and take forward key aspects of social care workforce development.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) how many social care packages in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust since April 2021 were reviewed; and (ii) how many of these reviewed packages had a (a) reduction; and (b) increase in time allocated.
answer_text: This information is not held centrally so the information was requested from Northern Health and Social Care Trust (HSCT). Northern HSCT advised that the information requested is not available from a system and could only be obtained by a manual count. This would be a considerable undertaking across all service areas and could therefore only be supplied at a disproportionate cost.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) what measures have been introduced; and (ii) what discussions have taken place, to allow a more flexible approach to applications for Blue Badges due to the delay with Personal Independence Payment awards.
answer_text: My Department is aware of issues the Department for Communities (DfC) is having when carrying out reassessments for people on short term Personal Independence Payments (PIP) awards. As a short-term measure, DfC are granting PIP extensions and when the Department’s Blue Badge Unit receives a renewal application supported by evidence that the applicant’s current PIP award has been extended, then a new badge can be issued for the period of the extension. As an alternative, applicants with a short-term PIP award are advised that they can apply under Section C1 (Assessed Criteria) in the absence of a current PIP award or a short-term extension. A three-year Blue Badge may be issued to any applicants who qualify upon completion of this assessment.Discussions had taken place between DfC and my officials about this issue and we have been advised that the issue of PIP extension letters should be resolved quite soon. You may however wish to engage directly with DfC on this matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the number of students who (i) applied to study for an undergraduate medicine course; (ii) started the undergraduate medicine course; and (iii) completed the academic year studying undergraduate medicine, in Northern Ireland, for each of the last three academic years.
answer_text: My Department does not hold information regarding applications to, nor completions of, higher education courses. We do, however, collect information on higher education ‘enrolments’ and ‘qualifications achieved’.As such, Table 1 below sets out first-year undergraduate enrolments on Medicine courses at Northern Ireland higher education institutions by domicile from 2018/19 to 2020/21.Table 1: EnrolmentsYearNIGBInternationalTotal2020/2121035503002019/2018535452702018/191952550270Table 2 below sets out undergraduate qualifiers from Medicine courses at Northern Ireland higher education institutions by domicile from 2018/19 to 2020/21.Table 2: QualificationsYearNIGBInternationalTotal2020/2116560402652019/2014560452502018/191754040260

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what is the current maximum capacity for undergraduate medicine places in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My Department supports the provision of 236 undergraduate medical training places at Queen’s University Belfast, and 70 undergraduate medical training places at Ulster University, per annum.In addition, QUB admits an average of 26 international students per annum, plus a further 9 students, who enter the course at third year, from the International Medical University in Malaysia. UU is able to admit up to 7 international medical students per annum.
